title: Homer Loves Flanders

jokes.text: The government calls it the army. But a more alarmist name would be: The Kill-bot Factory
jokes.setup: News report about military training being presented as scary
jokes.punchline: Ridiculous alternate name 'Kill-bot Factory' for the army
jokes.explanation: Sharp satirical take on media fearmongering with an absurd sci-fi twist. Economical and memorable.

jokes.text: Warning: Tickets should not be taken internally. See? Because of me, now they have a warning.
jokes.setup: Standard warning about football tickets
jokes.punchline: Anchor takes credit for causing the warning by presumably eating tickets
jokes.explanation: Clever meta-joke about warning labels and personal responsibility with absurd implication.

jokes.text: Those aren't tickets to the game, Homer. 'Free wig with every purchase of large wig. Downtown Wig Center.'
jokes.setup: Bart selling Homer what appear to be game tickets
jokes.punchline: They're actually wig store coupons with absurd offer
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment - Bart's schemes and Homer's gullibility. Absurd wig offer adds extra layer.

jokes.text: Why, you little-- Hmm. Free wig.
jokes.setup: Homer about to strangle Bart for tricking him
jokes.punchline: Gets distracted by the prospect of a free wig
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character comedy - Homer's anger instantly deflated by simple material desire. Perfect economy.

jokes.text: [Imitating Marge] I love you, Homie. Mmm. I don't need her at all anymore.
jokes.setup: Homer trying on the wig
jokes.punchline: Believes he can replace his wife with a wig and self-love
jokes.explanation: Dark and absurd character moment revealing Homer's shallow understanding of relationships.

jokes.text: I did it. Second in line. And all I had to do was miss eight days of work.
jokes.setup: Character proudly announcing his achievement
jokes.punchline: The 'achievement' cost him a week's worth of wages
jokes.explanation: Good character-specific joke about misplaced priorities and poor math skills.

jokes.text: With the money you would have made working, you could've bought tickets from a scalper. In theory, yes. Jerk.
jokes.setup: Marge points out the obvious flaw in his logic
jokes.punchline: He acknowledges she's right but insults her anyway
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment - Comic Book Guy's stubbornness and social awkwardness.

jokes.text: Give me, uh, 30,000 tickets. That'll be $950,000 please. Look, the thing about that is I only got $10 on me. Can I pay you the rest later?
jokes.setup: Man ordering an absurd number of tickets
jokes.punchline: Expects to buy almost a million dollars worth on credit with $10
jokes.explanation: Absurd escalation of greed and financial delusion, typical Simpsons background character.

jokes.text: That was Bobby McFerrin's new one, 'I'm Worried, Need Money.'
jokes.setup: DJ introducing a song
jokes.punchline: Parody title inverting McFerrin's optimistic 'Don't Worry Be Happy'
jokes.explanation: Sharp cultural reference joke that inverts a famous song's optimism into financial anxiety.

jokes.text: Ho-ho. Stupid sheep.
jokes.setup: Homer watching people rush for phones
jokes.punchline: Calls them sheep while doing the exact same thing
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer hypocrisy moment - no self-awareness while criticizing others.

jokes.text: Ned Flanders. Oh, not Flanders. Anybody but Flanders.
jokes.setup: Radio announces winner
jokes.punchline: Homer's despair that his neighbor won
jokes.explanation: Perfect setup for the episode's central relationship. Homer's specific dread of Ned winning is character-perfect.

jokes.text: Oh, golly. If that doesn't put the 'shaz' in 'shazam.' Oh, listen. What's the cash value of those tickets so I can report it on my income tax?
jokes.setup: Ned winning radio contest
jokes.punchline: Immediately asks about tax implications of free tickets
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ned Flanders characterization - his unique speech patterns and obsessive rule-following. The 'shaz/shazam' is pure Ned.

jokes.text: [Homer joins in singing 'I've got two tickets to paradise' with increasing desperation]
jokes.setup: Song about having tickets plays on radio
jokes.punchline: Homer desperately sings along despite not having tickets
jokes.explanation: Good cringe comedy showing Homer's delusion and desperation through song.

jokes.text: Why am I such a loser? Why? Well, your father was a loser, and his father, and his father. It's genetic, man.
jokes.setup: Homer lamenting his failure
jokes.punchline: Bart matter-of-factly explains it's hereditary with brutal honesty
jokes.explanation: Dark family comedy with Bart's casual cruelty toward his father. Generational failure as genetic trait is a strong concept.

jokes.text: They built a mini-mall, so we built a bigger mini-mall. They made the world's largest pizza, so we burnt down their city hall.
jokes.setup: Explaining Springfield-Shelbyville rivalry with normal competition
jokes.punchline: Sudden escalation to arson as competitive response
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation joke. Perfect structure building from mundane competition to criminal overreaction. Very quotable.

jokes.text: Yeah, they swore they'd get us back by spiking our water supply. But they didn't have the guts.
jokes.setup: Discussing Shelbyville's threatened revenge
jokes.punchline: Implies they're cowards for not poisoning the water
jokes.explanation: Dark comedy about treating mass poisoning as cowardly rather than merciful.

jokes.text: Ohh, the walls are melting again. Personally I think I'm overdone.
jokes.setup: Previous mention of water supply poisoning
jokes.punchline: Barney casually experiencing hallucinations and comparing himself to overcooked food
jokes.explanation: Brilliant payoff revealing the water WAS poisoned. Barney's casual food metaphor for his condition is perfect character comedy.

jokes.text: God, if you really are God...you'll get me tickets to that game. [Doorbell rings] Hidilly-ho, neighbor. Wanna go to the game with me?
jokes.setup: Homer prays for tickets
jokes.punchline: Ned immediately appears with tickets, but Homer doesn't see it as answered prayer
jokes.explanation: Classic ironic answered prayer joke with perfect timing. Homer's failure to recognize the miracle is character-appropriate.

jokes.text: Why do you mock me, O Lord? Homer, that's not God. That's just a waffle that Bart tossed up there.
jokes.setup: Homer addressing the ceiling dramatically
jokes.punchline: He's been praying to a waffle stuck on the ceiling
jokes.explanation: Absurd visual gag undermining Homer's spiritual moment. The mundane explanation for his 'divine' experience is perfect.

jokes.text: Mmm. I know I shouldn't eat thee, but-- [Growling] Mmm. 'Sacrilicious.'
jokes.setup: Homer eating the waffle he thought was God
jokes.punchline: Made-up word combining 'sacrilegious' and 'delicious'
jokes.explanation: One of The Simpsons' greatest made-up words. Perfect Homer moment combining gluttony with mock spirituality. Became a cultural touchstone.

jokes.text: Are you planning to hit Ned Flanders with that pipe and take his tickets? Ye-- No.
jokes.setup: Marge asking about Homer's suspicious behavior
jokes.punchline: Homer almost admits to violent plan before catching himself
jokes.explanation: Great character moment showing Homer's inability to lie effectively. The near-confession is perfectly timed.

jokes.text: Well, get out the Crayolas and color me tickled pink.
jokes.setup: Homer asking to go to the game
jokes.punchline: Ned's overly wholesome expression of excitement
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ned Flanders dialogue - wholesome, childlike expressions that reveal his character.

jokes.text: Ooh, what's with the lead pipe? Were you gonna give my noggin a floggin'? Well, yeah. [Both laugh]
jokes.setup: Ned innocently asking about the weapon
jokes.punchline: Homer admits violent intent and they both laugh
jokes.explanation: Dark comedy made light by Ned's innocent phrasing and both characters' casual reaction to violence.

jokes.text: Duck! I can't let the boys see me with you! Hey! Look, Homer's got one of those robot cars. One of those American robot cars.
jokes.setup: Homer hiding from friends while with Ned
jokes.punchline: Friends misinterpret his ducking as having a self-driving car, then specify 'American' after crash
jokes.explanation: Clever misdirection joke with social commentary on American car quality. The 'American' clarification after the crash is perfectly timed.

jokes.text: Men, there's a little crippled boy sitting in the hospital who wants you to win this game. I know because I crippled him myself to inspire you.
jokes.setup: Typical inspirational sports speech about sick child
jokes.punchline: Burns admits to deliberately injuring the child for motivation
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark comedy subverting sports movie tropes. Perfect Burns characterization - casual evil presented as motivational strategy.

jokes.text: Well, I hope they win, or Mr. Burns said he's coming back.
jokes.setup: Player responding to Burns' motivation
jokes.punchline: Implies Burns will injure more children if they lose
jokes.explanation: Good callback extending the dark joke about Burns' motivational methods.

jokes.text: What the hell happened to my dogs? I want answers.
jokes.setup: Homer ordering hot dogs at the game
jokes.punchline: They're apparently completely mangled or missing
jokes.explanation: Standard visual gag about stadium food quality. Homer's outrage is character-appropriate.

jokes.text: 'Nacho, nacho man' 'I want to be a nacho man'
jokes.setup: Homer wearing a nacho hat
jokes.punchline: Parodying 'Macho Man' with 'nacho' while wearing absurd food hat
jokes.explanation: Simple pun joke with visual component. Fits Homer's character but not particularly clever.

jokes.text: Stan, Stan, he's our boy. If he can't do it, no one... will.
jokes.setup: Homer starting an enthusiastic cheer
jokes.punchline: Loses enthusiasm and ends with pessimistic conclusion
jokes.explanation: Great character moment showing Homer's natural pessimism undermining his attempted enthusiasm.

jokes.text: We won! We won, Flanders! We won! [kissing Ned] Losers! Losers! Kiss my big Springfield behind, Shelbyville!
jokes.setup: Homer celebrating the victory
jokes.punchline: Goes from kissing Ned to aggressively taunting opponents
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Homer's emotional extremes and poor impulse control.

jokes.text: Hey, somebody sign an autograph. Please, somebody. Sign an autograph? Hey, would you sign-- You fat, stupid jerks!
jokes.setup: Player desperately seeking autograph seekers
jokes.punchline: Insults potential fans when they ignore him
jokes.explanation: Good reversal of typical athlete-fan dynamic with player becoming hostile when ignored.

jokes.text: I used to party all night and sleep with lingerie models until Ned and his Bible group showed me that I could have more. Professional athletes-- always wantin' more.
jokes.setup: Stan describing how Ned saved him from partying and models
jokes.punchline: Homer's sarcastic observation that athletes always want more
jokes.explanation: Great ironic commentary on religious conversion. Homer's deadpan response perfectly captures the absurdity.

jokes.text: Now I have four children. You will be called 'Scarface.'
jokes.setup: Homer receiving football as gift
jokes.punchline: Treats it as his child and gives it a violent nickname
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Homer moment combining his love of objects with inappropriate naming. The contrast between parental affection and violent nickname is perfect.

jokes.text: What did he say? I don't know. Something about being gay.
jokes.setup: Homer loudly declaring Ned as his friend
jokes.punchline: Crowd mishears 'friend' declaration as coming out
jokes.explanation: Simple misunderstanding joke typical of background character reactions.

jokes.text: Dad and Ned Flanders friends? Heh. What's next? A's on Bart's report card? Hey!
jokes.setup: Lisa expressing disbelief at impossible scenario
jokes.punchline: Compares it to Bart getting good grades, offending him
jokes.explanation: Good sibling dynamic joke with Lisa's academic superiority and Bart's defensive reaction.

jokes.text: They don't call me Springfield Fats just because I'm morbidly obese.
jokes.setup: Homer boasting about his pool skills
jokes.punchline: Implies nickname is for pool skills while explicitly stating it's about his weight
jokes.explanation: Great Homer self-awareness joke. He contradicts himself while trying to sound impressive.

jokes.text: Now you got a lawsuit on your hands. Just kidding.
jokes.setup: Homer appearing to threaten legal action after accident
jokes.punchline: Reveals he's joking, showing he's not actually litigious
jokes.explanation: Mild misdirection joke that fits character but isn't particularly strong.

jokes.text: Oh, don't you see? You do have a sin to confess. The sin of envy.
jokes.setup: Child wanting to confess but having no sin
jokes.punchline: Ned finds a sin in the child's desire to have sins to confess
jokes.explanation: Clever religious logic joke perfectly suited to Ned's character. The circular nature of finding sin in wanting sin is sophisticated.

jokes.text: Sure didelly do. Over 230 channels locked out.
jokes.setup: Homer asking about satellite dish
jokes.punchline: Ned has satellite but blocks almost everything
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ned Flanders joke about his censorious nature. Having technology but defeating its purpose is character-perfect.

jokes.text: Good night, Daddy! Good night, Uncle Homer!
jokes.setup: Children saying goodnight
jokes.punchline: They've adopted Homer as family despite his obvious discomfort
jokes.explanation: Sweet character moment that shows how the relationship affects the children.

jokes.text: Hey, I don't want no people in here with their 'evils of alcohol' rap.
jokes.setup: Ned being introduced at Moe's
jokes.punchline: Barney preemptively objects to temperance message
jokes.explanation: Good Barney character moment showing his defensive attitude toward his drinking.

jokes.text: 'And truly she was my friend Flicka.' [Sniffs] If this gets out, the next words you say will be muffled by your own butt!
jokes.setup: Barney emotionally moved by children's story
jokes.punchline: Threatens violence if his emotional moment is revealed
jokes.explanation: Great Barney moment showing his hidden sensitivity and masculine posturing. The threat is creatively phrased.

jokes.text: Can't talk. See Flanders. Later sex.
jokes.setup: Marge trying to talk to Homer
jokes.punchline: Homer's awkward abbreviation of 'later, sex' as goodbye
jokes.explanation: Funny character moment showing Homer's crude efficiency and obsession with Ned.

jokes.text: Uh, yeah. A judge made me do that once too. Stupid lack of public urinals.
jokes.setup: Ned mentioning charity work
jokes.punchline: Homer reveals his charity work was court-ordered for public urination
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer moment contrasting his crude legal troubles with Ned's genuine charity. The casual tone makes it funnier.

jokes.text: Oh, come on! We've been here for 15 minutes. Can't you see they're sucking the life out of us?
jokes.setup: Homer at charity event helping people
jokes.punchline: Complains that helping others is draining him
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Homer's selfishness and impatience with actual charity work.

jokes.text: Ah, for some reason, Moe's always closed on Wednesdays.
jokes.setup: Ned suggesting Homer go to Moe's instead
jokes.punchline: Homer doesn't know why his regular bar is closed one day a week
jokes.explanation: Mild character joke about Homer's lack of awareness of basic business practices.

jokes.text: Come on. Come on! Just give 'em the slop and let's get out of here. Oh, I can do it faster than that! Mmm, mmm. Mmm, mmm.
jokes.setup: Homer wanting to leave charity work quickly
jokes.punchline: Starts eating the food meant for charity recipients
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer moment combining impatience with gluttony. Visual gag of eating charity food is character-perfect.

jokes.text: Wow! Look at that enthusiasm! This is front-page material.
jokes.setup: Homer messily slopping food for homeless
jokes.punchline: Photographer thinks it's genuine enthusiasm worth publicizing
jokes.explanation: Misinterpretation of Homer's impatience as charitable enthusiasm

jokes.text: Oh, of course you'd say something like that, Marge. You've hated Ned for years. In fact, you wanted to bash his head in with a pipe. That was you!
jokes.setup: Homer deflecting Marge's concern
jokes.punchline: Projects his own violent thoughts onto Marge, then gets corrected
jokes.explanation: Good callback to earlier pipe joke with Homer's psychological projection and quick correction.

jokes.text: The Flanders are a bunch of geeks, man. The 'Flandereses' are not geeks!
jokes.setup: Bart calls the Flanders family geeks
jokes.punchline: Homer defends them while struggling with plural form
jokes.explanation: Good Homer grammar struggle while defending his new obsession

jokes.text: Don't worry, Bart. It seems like every week something odd happens to the Simpsons. My advice is to ride it out, make an occasional smart-aleck quip, and by next week, we'll be back to where we started from, ready for another wacky adventure.
jokes.setup: Bart worried about family changes
jokes.punchline: Lisa breaks fourth wall describing sitcom formula
jokes.explanation: Brilliant meta-commentary on sitcom conventions. Lisa's academic analysis of their own show format is sophisticated and funny.

jokes.text: Don't worry, Bart. It seems like every week something odd happens to the Simpsons. My advice is to ride it out, make an occasional smart-aleck quip, and by next week, we'll be back to where we started from, ready for another wacky adventure.
jokes.setup: Bart worries about the family dynamics changing
jokes.punchline: Lisa breaks the fourth wall about sitcom formula
jokes.explanation: Brilliant meta-commentary on sitcom reset conventions delivered naturally by Lisa

jokes.text: 'Well, well, well' 'W-W-W-W-W-W-Well, well, well' You know something? He did say 'well' a lot.
jokes.setup: Listening to Reagan rap tape
jokes.punchline: Homer's obvious observation about the repetitive lyrics
jokes.explanation: Mild political humor and character moment showing Homer's obvious observations.

jokes.text: Don't hog it all, smelly head! Go to hell, zit face! Give me that sugar!
jokes.setup: Flanders boys given sugar
jokes.punchline: Normally sweet children become violent and crude
jokes.explanation: Great character reversal showing the Flanders children becoming Bart-like when given sugar. Perfect corruption comedy.

jokes.text: If this were a more perfect world, we'd all be known as the Flimpsons.
jokes.setup: Homer toasting the friendship
jokes.punchline: Combines family names into awkward portmanteau
jokes.explanation: Good wordplay joke showing Homer's attempt at sentimentality. 'Flimpsons' is awkward enough to be funny.

jokes.text: Todd's got Zesty Italian in his eye. Okay. But at least let me take the boat out for one last spin. Oh, I don't know. Too late!
jokes.setup: Ned wanting to leave due to child's injury
jokes.punchline: Homer ignores the situation and steals the boat
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Homer's selfishness and inability to read social situations.

jokes.text: Oh, Lord, please give me the strength to endure Homer Simpson's friendship.
jokes.setup: Ned dealing with Homer's behavior
jokes.punchline: Prayers for strength to endure friendship rather than embrace it
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ned moment showing his religious framework struggling with Homer's chaos. The prayer format makes it funnier.

jokes.text: Hmm, your car. Boy, what are the odds, huh?
jokes.setup: Homer has obviously crashed Ned's car
jokes.punchline: Acts like it's a coincidence or bad luck
jokes.explanation: Good Homer character moment showing his refusal to accept responsibility with false casualness.

jokes.text: Marge... I think I hate Ted Koppel. No, wait. I find him informative and witty. Night.
jokes.setup: Ned having crisis about hating someone
jokes.punchline: Realizes he was thinking of wrong person and actually likes them
jokes.explanation: Great Ned character moment showing his struggle with negative emotions and quick self-correction.

jokes.text: Marge... I think I hate Ted Koppel. No, wait. I find him informative and witty. Night.
jokes.setup: Homer seems to be having same crisis as Ned about hating someone
jokes.punchline: Actually reconsidering TV host, not experiencing moral crisis
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection showing Homer's shallow concerns vs. Ned's moral struggle

jokes.text: Right. No reporters. No. I-- I-- I mean just the Flanders family.
jokes.setup: Ned trying to exclude Homer politely
jokes.punchline: Homer misunderstands 'family only' as meaning no reporters
jokes.explanation: Good cringe comedy showing Ned's politeness backfiring and Homer's obliviousness.

jokes.text: Lies make Baby Jesus cry.
jokes.setup: Ned about to lie to avoid Homer
jokes.punchline: Child's innocent religious guilt trip
jokes.explanation: Perfect Flanders family moment. Child's innocent religious teaching prevents parent's white lie.

jokes.text: Come on, Ned! Move this thing! I can't! It's a Geo!
jokes.setup: High-speed chase scene
jokes.punchline: Car chase limited by driving economy car
jokes.explanation: Great subversion of chase scene tropes. Ned's practical car choice undermining dramatic moment is perfect.

jokes.text: I told you, Officer. I'm not hepped up on goofballs. Yeah, right. Ned Flanders. I never would have imagined.
jokes.setup: Ned arrested and claiming innocence
jokes.punchline: Officer's disbelief that wholesome Ned could be a drug user
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing how Ned's reputation makes him unbelievable as a drug user.

jokes.text: High as a kite, everybody! Goofballs! Yeah, where's your messiah now, Flanders?
jokes.setup: Officer announcing Ned's arrest
jokes.punchline: Officer mocking Ned's faith during humiliation
jokes.explanation: Cringe humor showing public humiliation and religious mockery, fits the awkward situation.

jokes.text: [Homer's nose whistling during silent prayer]
jokes.setup: Solemn church prayer moment
jokes.punchline: Homer's loud nose whistling disrupting silence
jokes.explanation: Standard physical comedy disrupting solemn moment. Fits Homer's character as unintentional disruptor.

jokes.text: Stop it! Breathe through your damn mouth!
jokes.setup: Ned's frustration building during Homer's disruption
jokes.punchline: Ned finally snaps and curses in church
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment - Ned's religious composure finally cracking. The contrast with his usual demeanor makes it powerful.

jokes.text: Let's sacrifice him to our God! Come on. We did it all the time in the '30s.
jokes.setup: Congregation turning on Homer
jokes.punchline: Casual reference to 1930s human sacrifice as precedent
jokes.explanation: Dark historical humor treating human sacrifice as routine church practice. Absurdist escalation works well.

jokes.text: If everyone here were like Ned Flanders, there'd be no need for heaven. We'd already be there.
jokes.setup: Homer defending Ned to angry congregation
jokes.punchline: Profound observation about Ned's goodness from unlikely source
jokes.explanation: Beautiful character moment showing Homer's hidden wisdom and genuine appreciation. Touching and funny simultaneously.

jokes.text: Guess what, everyone! My Great-uncle Boris died and left us his old country house! There's only one catch. They say it's haunted. But I'm sure we can prove 'em wrong by spending the weekend there.
jokes.setup: Lisa's prediction about return to normal being wrong
jokes.punchline: Homer announces new 'wacky adventure' setup immediately
jokes.explanation: Brilliant meta-joke proving Lisa's sitcom analysis wrong by immediately setting up horror movie parody. Perfect callback structure.

jokes.text: Hididilly-ho, neighbor! Get lost, Flanders. Okilly-dokilly.
jokes.setup: Ned appearing for new adventure
jokes.punchline: Homer immediately back to hating him, Ned accepts cheerfully
jokes.explanation: Perfect reset to status quo completing Lisa's sitcom analysis. Ned's cheerful acceptance of rejection is character-perfect.

tmdb_overview: Flanders' extra ticket to the big game makes him Homer's new best friend, a role that sorely tests Flanders' patience.
insight: Homer-Flanders dynamic drives 71 jokes in 22 minutes, sustaining mid-tier consistency.
